title: Conjecture 1.1 (Aravind)
statement_text: Let $G$ be a triangle-free graph. Then for every colouring (not necessarily optimal) of $G$, there is a rainbow induced subgraph isomorphic to a $\chi(G)$-vertex path.
context_text: This conjecture, due to Aravind (cited via Babu, Basavaraju, Chandran and Francis), asks for the strongest possible rainbow induced path result in triangle-free graphs. It remains open; partial results include the case $\chi(G)=4$ and a version for graphs whose girth equals their chromatic number.
paper_contribution: Proves Theorem 1.3, a weaker statement in which the path length $s$ is fixed in advance rather than equal to $\chi(G)$: for every $\kappa, s \geq 1$ there exists $c$ such that every coloured graph $(G,\phi)$ with $\omega(G)\leq\kappa$ and $\chi(G)>c$ contains a rainbow induced $s$-vertex path. The authors also explicitly state they do not believe Conjecture 1.1, and report an unsuccessful search for a counterexample on the Mycielski graph with $\chi(G)=5$.
paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. IX. Rainbow paths

title: Question (rainbow hole with $s$ consecutive rainbow vertices)
statement_text: Is it true that for all fixed $s$, $\kappa$, if $G$ is a graph with $\omega(G)\leq\kappa$ and $\chi(G)$ sufficiently large, then in every colouring of $G$ there is a hole in which some set of $s$ consecutive vertices is rainbow?
context_text: The authors show that the shift graph of triples (coloured by middle elements) rules out a fully rainbow hole and even a hole in which every three consecutive vertices are rainbow. The weaker question of $s$ consecutive rainbow vertices in a hole is left open.
notes: Stated as prose in the Conclusion with 'we do not know the following'.
paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. IX. Rainbow paths

title: Question (uniquely-covered vertices in an induced path)
statement_text: Let $G$ be a triangle-free graph with very large chromatic number, and let $\mathcal{A}$ be a set of stable subsets (not necessarily pairwise disjoint) of $V(G)$ with union $V(G)$. Does there necessarily exist an $s$-vertex induced path $P$ of $G$ such that for each $v\in V(P)$, some $X\in\mathcal{A}$ satisfies $X\cap V(P)=\{v\}$?
context_text: The authors pose this as an additional open question in the Conclusion, asking whether a large chromatic number forces an induced path whose vertices are each 'uniquely covered' by some member of an arbitrary stable-set cover.
notes: Stated as prose in the Conclusion with 'another question to which we do not know the answer'.
paper_title: Induced subgraphs of graphs with large chromatic number. IX. Rainbow paths
